coredata.dc:title: Preparation of coal-based carbon quantum dots and their fluorescence properties

coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Wenyuan
He, Hongyu
Wang, Cheng
Zhang, Yonglong
Lu, Yunfei
Zhang, Qiang
Wang, Suhua
Zhang, Xianmei
Hao, Xiangyang
Sun, Mingtai
coredata.dc:description: In the field of life sciences, cellular movements play a significant role in influencing the activities of organisms. Current methodologies have limitations in the monitoring of cell recognition, biological macromolecule localization, cell labeling, migration, and biosensors. Although metal–semiconductor quantum dots are commonly used, they may pose toxicity risks to the lungs and kidneys. On the other hand, carbon quantum dots possess the unique optoelectronic properties of traditional quantum dots while exhibiting low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent photostability, and abundant raw material sources. In this research, carbon quantum dots were synthesized using the nitric acid oxidation method. The study investigated the effects of temperature, time, and the type of low-rank coal on the synthesis of carbon quantum dots. The structure and properties of the carbon quantum dots, specifically their fluorescence characteristics, were thoroughly analyzed. The proposed synthesis approach includes the nitration of coal macromolecules and the thickening of aromatic clusters through the Scholl reaction, leading to enhanced fluorescence quantum efficiency. The findings of this study indicate that the carbon quantum dots produced through this method demonstrate a high fluorescence quantum yield, making them ideal fluorescent agents for material preparation and offering potential applications in the field of life sciences.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Nitric acid oxidation
Fluorescence quantum yield (QY)
Scholl reaction
Low-rank coal

Characterization of carbon elements, structures, and morphology of CQDs
Electrostatic spinning, scanning electron microscopy, and fluorescence detection
Scholl reaction and related theoretical speculation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS characterization of precipitate 3 prepared under sample reaction conditions; (b) XRD of carbon quantum dots prepared from Longkou coal sample.
(a) Physical phase analysis of suspension 1 performed corresponding to sample; (b) Physical phase analysis of sediment 1 performed corresponding to sample.

(a) Raman spectra of the product prepared under experimental conditions of the sample; (b) lateral test of suspension 3 made from the sample; (c) the three-dimensional pattern of suspension 3 made from the sample;(d) transmission electron microscope test map of product precipitate 3 made from the sample.
(a) SEM image of the electrospun supernatant of Precipitation 3; (b) fluorescence spectrum of the electrospinning fluid.
Schematic diagram of the Scholl reaction of low-rank coal under acidic conditions, which may undergo dehydrogenative cyclization.
Comparison of the fluorescence quantum yields of coal-based CQDs and other CQDs.

row.entry: Coal-based CDs oxidized with hydrogen peroxide

head.ce:title: Preparation of coal-based carbon quantum dots and their fluorescence properties

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: W. Jiang and H. He contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the field of life sciences, cellular movements play a significant role in influencing the activities of organisms. Current methodologies have limitations in the monitoring of cell recognition, biological macromolecule localization, cell labeling, migration, and biosensors. Although metal–semiconductor quantum dots are commonly used, they may pose toxicity risks to the lungs and kidneys. On the other hand, carbon quantum dots possess the unique optoelectronic properties of traditional quantum dots while exhibiting low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent photostability, and abundant raw material sources. In this research, carbon quantum dots were synthesized using the nitric acid oxidation method. The study investigated the effects of temperature, time, and the type of low-rank coal on the synthesis of carbon quantum dots. The structure and properties of the carbon quantum dots, specifically their fluorescence characteristics, were thoroughly analyzed. The proposed synthesis approach includes the nitration of coal macromolecules and the thickening of aromatic clusters through the Scholl reaction, leading to enhanced fluorescence quantum efficiency. The findings of this study indicate that the carbon quantum dots produced through this method demonstrate a high fluorescence quantum yield, making them ideal fluorescent agents for material preparation and offering potential applications in the field of life sciences.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-zero-dimensional particles with a carbon skeleton structure and a size of less than 10 nm [1–3]. They mainly exist in three forms: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and polymer quantum dots (PDs) [4]. CQDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, stable fluorescence, and various physicochemical properties [5–8]. These properties make CQDs promising candidates for a wide range of applications, including bioimaging [9], sensing [10], drug delivery, and optoelectronic devices. Many CQDs/semiconductor composites have been studied for photocatalytic water splitting and the photodegradation of organic pollutants [11–14]. In the 21st century, the true potential of quantum dots is expected to be realized in the realm of life sciences.

Especially, CQDs possess exceptional scintillation and photobleaching resistance properties [15], making them ideal fluorescence source materials for biosensors used in DNA detection [16], carbohydrates, vitamins, proteins, and other applications [17–19]. Studying the fluorescence properties of CQDs aids in optimizing preparation conditions and serves as a foundation for their application research. Unlike other fluorophores, CQDs have multiple luminescence centers [20,21], which may arise from the structure of the CQDs themselves or from surface functional groups introduced during preparation. It is suggested that the fluorescence emission mechanism is derived from delocalized π bonds in conjugated molecules [11,22]. Compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs possess numerous enhanced optical properties [3]. Their surface can be modified with various functionalities, and fluorescence regulation can be achieved through appropriate doping and surface functionalization, thereby improving fluorescence quantum efficiency [23–25].

The materials used to produce CQDs include fullerene [26], carbon fiber, charcoal [27], activated carbon [28], carbon black [29], candles [30], petroleum coke [31], plants, etc. Theoretically, any carbon-containing material can generate CQDs through pyrolysis [32], deposition [33], condensation [34], and other reactions. Traditional methods for synthesizing CQDs typically involve the use of expensive and toxic precursors, high temperatures, and complex procedures, limiting their scalability and practical applications. In contrast, coal, an abundant and low-cost natural resource, has been proposed as a promising precursor for the synthesis of CQDs due to its high carbon content and rich heteroatoms [35–38].

Coal-based CQDs offer several advantages over conventional CQDs, including cost-effectiveness [39], sustainability [40], and good stability [41,42]. However, the synthesis of coal-based CQDs from coal is still in its early stages, and there is a need for further research to optimize the synthesis process, understand the underlying mechanisms, and explore the potential applications of these novel materials. Chi’s group [43] reported on single-layer graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) prepared from coal, and found that as the degree of coalification increases, the yield of monolayer graphene quantum dots decreases [42,44].

In this study, Coal-based CQDs were prepared using nitric acid oxidation methods [45], primarily employing de-ashed low-rank coals from different regions. The study focused on optimizing the coal type, temperature, time, and other experimental conditions to determine the microstructure and particle size of the CQDs. The physical, chemical, and fluorescence properties of the CQDs were analyzed for characterization. The aim was to identify the optimal process parameters and evaluate the impact of coal type and process conditions on CQDs yield. Furthermore, the modifications involving PEG were investigated, and the application of CQDs in electrospinning and fluorescent ink formulations was explored. Based on characterization, the optimal preparation conditions were determined, and the microstructure and particle size of the CQDs were optimized accordingly.

Three different coal samples were selected for the study: Longkou deashed low-rank coal, Balougou deashed low-rank coal, and Shenhua low-rank coal. The first two coal samples required a 200-mesh sieve, while Shenhua low-rank coal required an 80–100 mesh sieve. All other substances used were of analytical purity. The primary reagents included concentrated nitric acid and sodium hydroxide.

X-ray diffraction was performed using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer from Bruker. Height distribution was analyzed using a Nanoscope IIIa atomic force microscope from Bruker. Raman spectra were measured using a HORIBA RM1000 spectrometer. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements were conducted with a JEM-2100 electron microscope from JEOL. X-ray diffraction for phase and quantitative analysis was performed with a Bruker X-ray powder diffractometer to determine the phase composition and crystal structure of the samples. Sample morphology was analyzed using an S-4800 scanning electron microscope from Hitachi. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) carbon spectra were obtained using a Bruker NMR spectrometer. The Spectrum 100 Fourier Infrared Spectrometer from PerkinElmer was utilized to assess the presence of organic matter in the calcined samples. Fluorescence (FL) spectra were collected using an F-4600 FL spectrophotometer from Hitachi High-Tech.

The coal samples were first washed, dried, and then crushed [46]. The specific process is illustrated in Fig. S1. A 0.5 g coal sample was added to 100 mL 5 mol/L of nitric acid solution, with the reaction conducted for about 12 h at approximately 130°C (the reaction solution changed from black to red after 8 h). The reaction solution was sonicated for 30 min to disperse the quantum dots evenly. The solution was then centrifuged at 4500 rpm to separate precipitate 1 and supernatant 1. After removing precipitate 1, the supernatant was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to obtain precipitate 2 and supernatant 2. The carbon quantum dots were collected from the supernatant, and 30 mL of secondary distilled water was added. The solution was adjusted to neutral with 1 mol/L sodium hydroxide and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to obtain the final supernatant 3 and precipitate 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different parameters on the fluorescence properties of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S2 a, it is observed that the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots in Shenhua coal samples was lower under the same experimental conditions, and the emission wavelength of the synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was inconsistent with the literature. At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of the underlying coal samples was also very low. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of Longkou ash coal is significantly higher, with lower impurity levels. Consequently, ash coal is chosen for subsequent experiments. Fig. S2 b shows that the emission wavelength at 130°C is more favorable than at 120 °C. The increase in temperature may enhance the oxidation of nitric acid in the coal sample, although the fluorescence intensity at 140 °C is not as strong as at 130 °C. It is speculated that the high temperature partially degrades the structure of carbon quantum dots, resulting in lower luminescence. Therefore, the experiments were conducted at 130 °C. Fig. S2c demonstrates that the image intensity is high, and the peak is normal. However, extending the reaction time to 14 h produces results comparable to the 13-hour reaction. We speculate that the extended reaction time may cause structural damage to carbon quantum dots when exposed to high temperatures over 100 °C. Due to the large number of experimental samples, this experiment represents three typical examples found through repeated fluorescence tests. The optimized experimental conditions are as follows: the reaction temperature is set at 130 °C, the reaction time is 12 h, and the coal sample used is Longkou ash coal. The samples will be prepared under these conditions for subsequent experiments such as structure and performance characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon elements, structures, and morphology of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a shows the X-ray energy spectrum of precipitation 3 prepared under the sample reaction conditions, with the binding energy peak 283.87 eV corresponding to sp2(C=C), 285.06 eV corresponding to C-OH, 287.54 eV corresponding to C=O, 288.23 eV corresponding to HO–C=O. From the XRD diagram of precipitation 3 and suspension in Fig. 1b, there are obvious characteristic peaks at 2θ = 28° and 2θ = 30°, where the peak of precipitation 3 is significantly higher than that of the suspension. From the above information, the possible functional groups of carbon quantum dots can be roughly observed.

Fig. 2 shows the physical phase analysis of suspension and precipitate derived from the preparation process were prepared under the following reaction conditions: reaction temperature of 130 °C, reaction time of 12 h, and using the Longke coal sample. The results exhibit that the suspension contains NaNO3, while the precipitate contains silicate, including SiO2. The presence of SiO2, a residue in the coal sample, indicates that nitric acid effectively cracks the coal sample and facilitates coal bond fracture. Additionally, the NaNO3 found in the suspension demonstrates the effectiveness of NaOH neutralization. These results suggest that the reaction conditions are optimal for extracting carbon quantum dots in this experimental setup.

As can be seen from the Fourier transform infrared spectra of precipitate 3 and suspension 3 (Fig. S3), the broad peak in the range of 3100 cm−1 to 3400 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the –OH group. The absorption peak near 1718 cm−1 to 1722 cm−1 is attributed to –COOH, while the peak at 1607 cm−1 indicates C=C bonds in the aromatic ring. The peak at 1400 cm−1 is associated with C-OH, and the peak at 916 cm−1 corresponds to the surface bending vibration of the O-H bond. In summary, the synthesized quantum dots have a significant number of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on their surface, which originate from the oxidation of coal-based carbon materials.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the D peaks (1360 cm−1) and G peaks (1580 cm−1) of the product suspension and precipitate were compared with those of the experimental samples. This comparison is a straightforward method to confirm the presence of carbon quantum dots, which are structurally similar to graphene. Nanoscope Analysis 1.8 was used to analyze the suspension prepared under the sample's reaction conditions, which revealed evenly distributed tiny particles. As depicted in Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c (in lateral mode and 3D mode, respectively), Despite an insufficient purification process, it is evident that the average diameter of most particles is about 10 nm, and the vertical height of 1.2 nm confirms them as carbon quantum dots. Fig. 3d shows TEM images of product precipitates prepared under the same conditions. The precipitate exhibited excellent dispersion, with nearly spherical, uniformly sized particles having a lattice size of about 0.5 nm and an average particle size of 5 nm. Additionally, the unique crystal structure is very similar to graphene, indicating that these quantum dots belong to the graphene quantum dot category.

The fluorescence quantum yield is an important method to characterize the fluorescence performance. It represents the fraction of the molecules that return to the ground state in the excited state, namely the ratio of the total energy of the emitted fluorescence to the absorbed energy. To compare the fluorescence properties of the CQDs prepared under different conditions, quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol/L sulfuric acid solution was used as a reference material to calculate the fluorescence quantum yield of the sample precipitate 3, and the test solution and quinine sulfate solution can be measured directly with and without oxygen removal at room temperature. Based on Eq. (1), the fluorescence quantum yield of the sample was calculated by measuring the integrated fluorescence intensity at the best and emission wavelength of the dilute excitation wavelength and the quinine sulfate in all experiments [47].
(1)
η = ε / α =
∫
L
e m i s si o n
/
∫
L
solvent
-
∫
L
sample
Where Lemission represents the emission spectrum of the sample, Esolvent represents the excitation spectrum of the sample, Esample represents the excitation spectrum of the solvent, α and ε represent the photon absorption values and the emission values of the sample, respectively.

According to the above equation in this paper, the fluorescence quantum yield of the sample (130 °C, 12 h, Longkou coal) is calculated to be 9.11 %. The fluorescence quantum yield of coal-based quantum dots (CQDs) was compared with that of other carbon quantum dots (QDs), and the results are presented in Table 1 . It can be observed that a relatively high fluorescence quantum yield is exhibited by the carbon quantum dots prepared in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrostatic spinning, scanning electron microscopy, and fluorescence detection
ce:section.ce:para: A specific mass of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was dissolved in deionized water containing 3 % glacial acetic acid, allowed to expand at room temperature for 1 h, and stirred at 90 °C for 1 h until completely dissolved, producing an 8 % mass fraction aqueous PVA solution. Then, the suspension was added and sonicated for another 30 min to ensure even dispersion. Finally, electrospinning was performed at a temperature of 20 °C, with 60 % humidity, a voltage intensity of 16 kV, a reception distance of 15 cm, and an injection speed of 0.02 ml/min [51].

Fig. 4 a is an SEM image of the electrospun supernatant of Precipitation 3. The figure demonstrates uniform filament formation with moderate thickness, suggesting that the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) did not interfere with the dissolution of PVA. Fig. 4b presents the fluorescence spectrum of the electrospinning fluid. This spectrum exhibits characteristic peaks that are relatively symmetric around the central axis. Overall, the thickness of the filaments indicates that incorporating CQDs into the PVA electrospun membranes creates a fluorescent film. This PVA membrane with fluorescent properties has potential applications. The incorporation of these fluorescent CQDs into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films presents significant potential for various applications due to their unique luminescent characteristics. The formation of electrospun films is heavily influenced by factors such as polymer concentration and molecular weight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scholl reaction and related theoretical speculation
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the existing literature, the preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through the reaction of coal and nitric acid involves several key functions:
Acidification: Nitric acid (HNO3) destroys weak covalent bonds such as ether and ester bonds in coal macromolecules. Since the amount of these bonds in the coal macromolecule skeleton is minimal, their destruction does not significantly damage the three-dimensional structure or dissolve the coal powder to the micron level.
Nitration: Nitric acid introduces nitro groups (–NO2) onto the benzene ring activated by the electron-donating groups of aliphatic hydrocarbons.
Oxidation: Under heating, HNO3 oxidizes the α-position carbon of the aromatic ring side chain to –COOH, causing fractures and ring opening of cyclic alkanes containing hydroxyl groups. This results in significant damage to the three-dimensional skeleton of coal macromolecules, macroscopically manifesting as the dissolution of the coal solid. However, the carbon–carbon bonds in the aromatic ring remain stable and do not break under these conditions.
Scholl Reaction: Dehydrogenation cyclization occurs under acidic conditions (or with Lewis acid), as illustrated in (Fig. 5 a, b, c).

It has been found that low-rank coal is more suitable for the preparation of single-layer graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) with an average size of 10 nm [43]. As the degree of coalification (carbon content from 66.36 % to 90.2 %) increases, the yield of S-GQDs decreases from 56.3 % to 16.44 %. High-rank coal is more suitable for carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs). According to the accepted Shinn model, low-rank coal has a low degree of graphitization and a small aromatic core, consisting of only 3–4 benzene rings (less than 0.5 nm). The total ring number of hexabenzocoronene is 13, with a diameter of 1.1 nm; polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PCAH) have 12 rings, with a diameter of 1–2 nm, as shown in (Fig. S4). Therefore, it can be inferred that carbon quantum dots are small molecules, not macromolecules. After the initial three well-known effects, coal macromolecules become smaller and do not form larger graphene-like structures.

We speculate that the reaction of low-rank coal with HNO3 undergoes a Scholl reaction and dehydrogenation cyclization between aromatic cores, forming a large aromatic cluster that eventually results in single-layer graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs). These S-GQDs are not only large aromatic systems (aromatic clusters) but also resemble graphene oxide (GO). Previous reports show that the various maps of GQDs are very similar to those of GO obtained from graphite oxidation [43]. In the current graphene research boom, the most commonly used preparation method is oxidation–reduction, where KClO4-HNO3 is used to produce GO containing hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which are then removed through reduction to obtain reduced graphene oxide (RGO). Similarly, coal-based graphene can be further prepared from S-GQDs. By utilizing China's abundant and inexpensive low-rank coal, we can reduce the predatory exploitation of graphite mines driven by the “graphene fever.” Additionally, it is safer to use KClO4-HNO3, a highly corrosive substance. By improving reaction and separation conditions, the yield, efficiency, and purity of coal-based graphene can be enhanced. This method is suitable for preparing quantum dots and their derivatives from raw materials with complex chemical structures.

After neutral membrane dialysis, the solution placed in a round-bottom flask was added 10–20 mg of passivation agent PEG1500N. The mixture was heated to 130 °C and refluxed for 72 h. After cooling, another neutral membrane dialysis was performed for 3 days (molecular weight cut-off: 3 kDa). The resulting solution with fluorescence characterization and fluorescent ink mapping (under ultraviolet light) is shown in (Fig. S5 a, b, c). The fluorescence representation diagram shows a more uniform peak compared to untreated carbon quantum dots. After PEG treatment, the carbon quantum dot solution exhibits a significant fluorescence intensity. When the fluorescent ink product is applied to paper and observed under ultraviolet light, a faint blue light is visible, indicating a positive effect.

Fig. S6 presents fluorescence images of samples without PEG treatment under excitation wavelengths of 532 nm (visible light) and 325 nm (ultraviolet light). Fig. S6a shows significant luminescence at 532 nm, with numerous bright luminous particles visible in the solution. Fig. S6b demonstrates that under 325 nm excitation, the substance emits blue fluorescence, indicating its inherent fluorescent properties and potential for application. In contrast, Fig. S6c and Fig. S6d show samples of carbon quantum dots treated with PEG and subjected to dialysis. Both images exhibit significantly increased brightness under both excitation wavelengths, further confirming the enhanced feasibility of the treatment.”

The optimal experimental conditions determined are 130 °C, using Longkou deashed coal samples, for 12 h. The method involves nitric acid oxidation, followed by neutral membrane dialysis for separation. Carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups are present on the surface of the carbon quantum dots, which have an average particle size of about 10 nm, similar to the structure of graphene. This discussion emphasizes the potential of converting low-rank coal into carbon quantum dots, providing a high-value utilization of low-rank coal. Among three different coal samples tested, Longkou deashed coal was found to be the most effective.

Characterizations of the surface functional groups of the carbon quantum dots suggest that the reaction involves the Scholl reaction, accompanied by an increase in benzene rings. Carbon quantum dots have potential applications in electrostatic spinning, while PEG-modified carbon quantum dots are suitable for fluorescent inks, both yielding favorable outcomes. The fluorescence quantum yield of PEG-modified and difluoromethane sulfonimide-treated carbon quantum dots can be enhanced. Given the fluorescence properties of carbon quantum dots, this study is significant for developing fluorescent materials.

Wenyuan Jiang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Hongyu He: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Cheng Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Yonglong Zhang: Software. Yunfei Lu: Conceptualization. Qiang Zhang: Conceptualization. Suhua Wang: Resources. Xianmei Zhang: Formal analysis. Xiangyang Hao: Writing – review & editing. Mingtai Sun: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176044, and U21A20290), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2024A1515010271), and the Projects of Talents Recruitment of GDUPT (2019rc056, and 2019rc057).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111818.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
